meta.title: OpenTechEvents — an open spec for tech community events
meta.description: An open specification to describe, publish and share tech community events. Publish once, be everywhere.

draft.body: The spec is not stable yet: fields can still change, be renamed or be dropped. The core is being decided right now.

hero.title: Publish your event once. Let it show up everywhere.
hero.lead: OpenTechEvents (OTE) is an open, free specification to describe tech community events — meetups, conferences, workshops, online or in person — in a single format that directories, calendars and tools can read automatically. It brings together the best of iCalendar, RSS and schema.org/Event, and stays compatible with all of them: write it once and we help you export it automatically to all three. Four formats for the price of one.
hero.note: No platform to sign up for. No tool to install: a file you publish, or a link in your site's <head>.
hero.shortcut: Already use Meetup, Luma, Eventbrite…? We'll help you import it without leaving it →

problem.title: You already know the problem
problem.lead: You organise one event. Then you announce it over and over, by hand, across several networks whose algorithms decide who even sees it. In today's attention economy, your message competes against all the noise — and you still miss half the people who would have come.

c1.title: The same event, announced again and again
c1.body: Your website, Meetup, LinkedIn, Instagram, X, several WhatsApp and Telegram groups… The same information on platforms where content can easily go unnoticed.

c2.title: Directories can't find you
c2.body: Event directories want to list you. But they'd have to find your event, or wait for you to submit a form, open a pull request, send an email, and so on… So they almost never do.

c3.title: Every change means doing it all over
c3.body: Venue moved. Speaker cancelled. New date. Now go update every single platform — or live with the stale copies out there.

problem.pull: There is no shortage of information about your events. What's missing is interoperability.

spec.title: One file. That's the whole idea.
spec.lead: You describe your events in a simple JSON file and publish it at a stable URL. Anything that speaks OTE — directories, calendars, bots, websites — can then read it without asking you for anything.

s1.title: You publish
s1.body: A feed with your events at a URL you control, e.g. yourcommunity.dev/events.json. And you can list yourself in our directory so people find you.

s2.title: Tools read it
s2.body: Aggregators, directories and converters can pick it up on their own — and turn it into RSS, iCalendar or schema.org.

s3.title: Each person picks their tool and filters
s3.body: Each person chooses the tool they prefer — calendar, email, RSS, a Telegram or WhatsApp bot — and the filters they care about.

s4.title: The right people find you
s4.body: It's not just that people find you: the right people do. With those filters you reach whoever is interested in the event, not a generic feed.

min.title: The minimum is genuinely minimal
min.body: A valid event needs little more than a name, a start date, a time zone and where it happens. Optional modules — CFP, speakers, price, accessibility — cover the rest when you need them.
min.caveat: Careful, because we'd rather be straight with you: that draft — the core fields and the optional modules alike — has not been agreed yet. It's there to show the shape of the thing and to open the debate, and it will change. What we're asking you to adopt today is the idea, and to come and break it before it sets.
min.b1: Plain JSON. No SDK, no account, no server required — a static file is enough.
min.b2: Designed to map cleanly onto RSS, iCalendar and schema.org/Event.
min.b3: Works the same for a recurring meetup, a multi-day conference or an online workshop.
min.b4: Open licence, no usage restrictions — in the spirit of RSS and iCal.
min.caption: Minimal valid event

docs.title: Read the full specification
docs.lead: Everything is public and in the open — including what's still undecided. The spec is at version 0.x: expect it to change, and expect your feedback to be what changes it.

d1.title: Field reference
d1.body: Every field of an event and of a feed: type, whether it's required, examples. Generated from the schemas, so it can't drift.

d2.title: Feed format
d2.body: How to group several events into a feed: metadata, pagination, licence and discovery.

d3.title: Examples
d3.body: Complete documents to copy: a minimal event, a meetup, a conference with an open CFP, and a full feed.

d4.title: Research
d4.body: The analysis behind the decisions: existing standards, platforms and directories we studied.

contrib.title: Something doesn't fit your community?
contrib.body: Then the spec is wrong, not your community. It's still a draft precisely so that organisers can push back before anything is set in stone — a missing field, a format that breaks your case, a name that makes no sense. Say so in an issue.

benefits.title: What you get out of it
benefits.lead: Adopting OTE is under an hour of work the first time. After that, a couple of minutes per event you publish. Here's what it buys you.

b1.title: Stop doing data entry
b1.body: Write the event once. Let tooling push it to directories, calendars and feeds instead of you copy-pasting it into yet another form.

b2.title: Reach beyond your own bubble
b2.body: Directories and aggregators will be able to pick up your events the moment you publish them, and reach each person in the channel they already use — not the one you happen to pick. Your meetup can show up where people are actually looking.

b3.title: Own your data
b3.body: Your events live at your URL, in an open format. If a platform shuts down, changes its API or its pricing, your history and your feed are still yours.

b4.title: An open, community-owned ecosystem
b4.body: Because OTE is an open spec owned by the community itself, it has the potential for a whole ecosystem of free, open integrations. Every new tool built on it — an .ics export, a website widget, a Telegram bot — works for you without you lifting a finger.

b5.title: Accurate everywhere
b5.body: Change the date in one place and every consumer that re-reads your feed gets the correction. No more zombie listings with the old venue.

b6.title: Shape the standard
b6.body: The spec is in draft. Early adopters decide what it looks like — if something doesn't fit your community, it can still be changed.

adopt.title: How to adopt it
adopt.lead: Three steps. The first one is the only one that takes real work, and it's a text file.

s1.title: Publish an OTE feed
s1.body: Create a JSON file with your events and host it anywhere you already publish — your site, GitHub Pages, a gist. The fastest path? The organizer kit: a ready-made repository to publish in minutes, with OTE, ICS and RSS feeds already wired up. And if your events live in a Google Calendar or Meetup, we'll help you import them to generate the feed.

s2.title: Make it discoverable
s2.body: Add one line to the <head> of your website so tools can find the feed on their own — the same way RSS readers do.
s2.caption: In your site's <head>

s3.title: Tell us, and get listed
s3.body: Open an issue with your feed URL. We'll validate it and list your community on this page; and as aggregators come online, they'll start picking up your events.

ics.tag: Not sure it's worth the effort? Read this first.
ics.title: Keep using what you already have, import it easily, improve it a little and get big benefits.
ics.body: No JSON to write, no tool to change: your events from Meetup, your .ics, Eventbrite… we help you import them as they are. By adding just a few more fields — topics (tags), attendance mode, language, coordinates — you multiply their reach.
ics.discovery: Those are the fields that let someone filter by tags, attendance mode or coordinates; and because it's an open, free standard —and because it isn't a specific platform or one with restricted APIs, but an RSS-style specification designed specifically to solve the problems of tech communities and events—, it's far easier for people to find you and subscribe to what interests them, just as other directories can give your event visibility with no effort.

adopters.title: Who publishes in OTE
adopters.lead: Communities and events already exposing their data in this format.
adopters.empty: The spec is brand new, so this list is still short. Be one of the first — early adopters get a say in what the standard becomes.
adopters.machine: This list is data, not markup: it renders adopters.json, a machine-readable registry you can build on. See the developer docs.

consumers.title: Who consumes OTE data
consumers.lead: Directories, aggregators and people reusing feeds published in this format. Every one of them is another place your events will be able to reach for free.
consumers.empty: Building a directory, a newsletter or a bot? Consume OTE feeds and get listed here.

tools.title: Ecosystem of tools
tools.lead: What exists today, and what's up for grabs. Claim one by opening an issue.

items.q: Does this compete with Meetup, Eventbrite or Luma?
items.a: No. OTE is a data format, not a platform. The point is to interoperate with those tools: describe the event once, then publish or convert it towards whatever platforms you already use.

items.q: Do I have to stop using my current tools?
items.a: No, not at all. The goal is for your data to flow easily to many more platforms — and, if you use several, to speed up the work of re-entering it in each one.

items.q: Does it replace RSS or iCalendar?
items.a: No. OTE is designed to be convertible to them. An OTE feed can be exported to RSS, JSON Feed or iCal so attendees consume it in their feed reader or calendar app.

However they prefer. Some want events in their calendar, others by email, others in a WhatsApp or Telegram group, others in an RSS reader or their social feeds. Because it's an open specification, each tool in the ecosystem can cover a different preference — no one has to bend to one platform's app. You publish once; each person picks their channel.

items.q: How is this different from schema.org/Event?
items.a: schema.org/Event isn't a rival to OTE, it's a mapping target: OTE serialises to JSON-LD almost 1:1 and can be exported to schema.org so Google and aggregators like dev.events pick you up. The difference is that schema.org describes one event embedded in one HTML page — there's no notion of a feed: you can't subscribe to «all of a community's events» or consume them without scraping HTML — its vocabulary is huge and almost entirely optional, and it ships no validation. OTE fills those gaps: a subscribable feed (one URL, N events, with an updatedAt) as a plain JSON file, a small validatable core, and online/hybrid and CFP as first-class fields. In one line: OTE = schema.org's data model + RSS/iCal's distribution model, without making you choose.

items.q: How much work is this really?
items.a: For a small community: writing one JSON file and adding a link tag to your site. If you already generate your events page from data, it's usually a matter of emitting one more file.
Not yet. We're at version 0.x and everything — name, fields, licence, governance — is open to debate. That's precisely why joining now is worth it: early adopters shape the format.

The proposal comes from Community Builders (ComBuildersES) and is now developed in its own home, the OpenTechEvents organisation on GitHub, with an international scope. It is not an official standard yet — it's an open, community-driven design effort, and it needs organisers like you to become one.

final.title: A standard is worth exactly as much as the number of people who use it
final.body: Every community that publishes an OTE feed makes the format more useful for every other community — and for everyone trying to find your events.

footer.note: An initiative by Community Builders. Draft specification — the name, scope and licence are still provisional.
